It's really hold you back from making, you know, the big money It's gonna also put you in a position where it's gonna be difficult to really be a successful entrepreneur and It's also one of the reasons why many people don't become entrepreneurs. They stay inside a box and it has to do with the mistaken perception that most people have that there's a shortage of money in the world that it's hard to raise money and Because of that because they think there's a shortage of money There's so many people out there. You might be one of them great ideas You know and yeah, and you want to grow a business you want to go into business for yourself You want to do something really extraordinary you have massive value to give But you just don't have that little bit of startup capital You need to turn the idea you had into a reality because you know, here's here's something that you know You hear quite often people say, you know, oh, you know, it's it's it's actually a limiting belief That you need to have money to make money You get it and say you don't need money to make money You can make money without having money and by the way, I'm here to tell you that's a bunch of shit It's not a limiting. It's fucking true. I don't money. It's always available People with money are always looking to deploy capital You just need to know the rules of how you go about raising money. Okay, but you need to understand the basics to really get this stuff Okay, so watch imagine you have 100% an idea of an idea So the basis of venture capital could be that you is that basically you can sell 50% of that idea You could sell 25% You could sell 20% you could sell 10% of the idea you can sell 5% of the idea you could choose What slice of the pie you want to sell to someone for a certain amount of money? So how would you calculate what that amount of money is well very simple? Let's say the idea the entire idea is worth $100,000 That's what that the cause of your ability of a good idea and because you're really good at explaining the idea People they people say that that's worth 100 grand. So let's say I want you to become my 50 50 partner Well, then how do we do that? Well, very simply I'd say you I'll tell you what I'll do I'm gonna give you a half my idea We'll form a company and I will incorporate or put my partnership and I'll take my idea We'll put it into that company and we'll build a business around that, right and I want you to give me $50,000. I want you to invest $50,000 Okay into the company, right? So you you put the idea into this company now watch this is very important here So you'll have this let's close. We'll use a corpora. We'll use a corporation. Okay, so you have this corporation a Right and his you over here You have your idea and then you have your investor over here Okay, so what happens if you want it? Let's say you want to raise $50,000. You need $50,000, right? And your idea is worth $100,000, right? So what happens? Your investor takes $50,000, right? He doesn't give you the $50,000 What happens is you put the idea into a corporation? Right and now you own 100% of that corporation you own the entire company, right? It's your company you form a company and you put your idea into this company It's legally the company has the idea right the rights to it, right? And then what happens is this person buys 50% of the company and puts their money into the company So their 50k goes into the company the company issues them shares So now you would have 50% of the shares and this person has 50% of the shares and your 5050 partners In other words when someone invests money that $50,000 it doesn't go into your pocket That's not a cash out financing Well, you actually say hey, I need 50 grand you take the 50 grand put in your pocket What do you do now to grow the company this person's giving you the 50 grand? Because they want you to invest it in the company to take this idea and turn it into a reality Does that make sense? So when you hear about things like cash out with some guy He see he raised venture capital the guy put 20 million in his pocket. That's not what happens It's a very different type of situation in that situation. We have a much more developed company, right? Let's say the let's say the company is worth Let's say it's this guy owns the company all right and the company's value of 20 million dollars And let's say so this person owns a show a different situation now watch So let's say there's This is the company company see right and this is you here You own 100% of the company or all the shares and let's say you've built the business now You have a business you think is worth 10 million dollars That's the value of your company right and let's say you need to raise you Let's say you want to sell 10% of this company 10% right? But here's the thing let's say you want only half a million dollars So if you it's worth 10 million dollars right 10% would be what one million dollars So if you sell this guy 10% of the company What happens well what happened is essentially that you could five percent could come from the company and five percent could come from you so you would take 500,000 dollars and put it in your pocket and 500,000 will go into the company coffers so 500,000 would go to the company 500,000 would go to you and The company would have 500,000 the bank to grow its business and you'd be personally $500,000 richer that's how you do a cash out financing Where you sell a piece of your company? But instead of the company issuing the shares the company issues half or some part of the shares and then you Issue the rest of the shares from you because you own 100 now you sell off your only on 95% You get it so you only own 95% of the company So you your your ownership goes down Essentially you know nice because you get diluted out because the company issues shares as well So you're you end up where you're 90% and they own 10. That's how you get cash in your pocket Okay, then there's some delusion. That's why it's a little bit more right so does that make sense to you So if you want to put money in your pocket when you're raising money, so only Some of the shares come from the company the rest of the shares come from you. Okay now here's the deal Often especially in these early stage companies these deals are not very doable Because if you go to someone that's wealthy and has money they just say what the fuck why would I give you money? I haven't pulled the salary in 24 months And now I've turned the corner and it's starting to really make money So what I'd like is like to get the money back I invest in so at least I got my risk off the table I get my back pay pay back my mother-in-law and then the rest goes down like okay. Why understand that? That makes sense So what actually is this what you need to this is the little tricks of the trade here is that when you're raising money? Even if you have a small company if you know what you're doing and you're gonna frame the idea You could put a few dollars in your pocket if you legitimately have Reasons why someone that's intelligent with money would say okay that makes sense I'm comfortable giving you a couple hundred thousand to put back in your pocket You deserve it for all the hard work you've done for the risk you've taken But I want most of the money going into the company so while it's not impossible To do this it is impossible if you don't know how to frame it the right way So if you want to pull money out for yourself You have to have a reason why it has to be part of the story and it's gotta be because you took the risk in the beginning You put it all out there you haven't pulled the salary Yeah, yeah, but and then people will be saying you know what that makes sense to me You deserve to get even good for you and you have the possibility. Okay, so that's step number one So this is just a very basics of how the mechanics of raising money work Now let's talk about how you go about Increasing the perceived value of your company and this is really where the magic lies in This is called like gorilla venture capital like so this is like venture capital that matters to you Not to like so I could teach you to become a venture capitalist or an MBA because that's you know works in a big bank That's not rational here, but in your own life I promise you and you're gonna thank me that I've given you this skill set here because One day in a probably not so distant future You're gonna be able to tap into this skill set right now practical Capital raising to take your life and your business or your career to the next level. All right, so let's talk about the reality now of how you take this idea and Make it more valuable So remember what I said, it's always about the idea multiplied by some What ability you have to tell the story so what are the AIDS the things that could help you tell the story so and so imagine this you have an idea and Then you have which is like the sort of proposal way something's gonna happen and then you have reality I Mean this is where you actually three years later You've achieved everything that you told this person your idea was you gonna build this new type of computer company and this and it all Worked and this is wow you're making 50 million in now. So this is reality So imagine this idea is worth X. Let's say we'll call an idea is worth $100,000 Right because you know an idea is what well by by its very nature when we look at ideas that kind of likes Say what's kind of half baked as the phrase goes meaning someone comes to you an idea and They explain I got this great. Yeah, I'm gonna do this with that. You're like, wow, it's a really interesting idea Do you have a business plan? Because if not on the business plan, you're like, well, it's kind of fucking you haven't really even thought it out yet Enough to put it on paper and done a marketing analysis or risk assessment I'm a balance sheet a pro former a cash flow model five-year projections or go to market strategy Your who else is gonna be part of your company? Yeah, you haven't thought the whole you haven't done that much It seems kind of half baked. So by their very nature by a very nature ideas in the eyes of investors like me are very half baked What makes them more real is when someone takes the next step Which is they write it down and Form what's called a business plan There's a very simple acceptable Template that people like myself who have been in venture capital you all expect to see of Idea if someone has an idea they want to sell me that if someone tells me no idea You know what chance they have of getting money from me? Zero unless it's my my child Okay, and then I'm very generous but other than my own child my own children. Okay, you're not getting money from me Just by telling me an idea What I'll say to you is oh, let me see the business plan and Then I will look at the business plan. I don't give a shit about it The business plan has no value, but without it. I would never give you money So the business plan that's what be clear with you about this. You're never gonna raise money Because you have a business plan But you can't raise money without it except from maybe your parents. Okay, I Expect that someone has gone far enough that they've taken a half-baked idea And they've written it down. They've thought it out. They've projected it out They've showed me the market analysis to go to marketing strategy They're balancing the cash flow the proceeds they need the use of proceeds what they get a user for who is that management Do the whole thing and by doing this if you take this idea and You take the time to write it all out and come with a really great a kick-ass business plan. Guess what? Suddenly the idea is multiplied by five It becomes worth $500,000 the perceived value to someone like myself Rises by a factor of as much as five or ten Simply because you took the time to take the idea and Write it down I'll tell you story It's a great story because you have no part of it the story of Steve Madden shoes So the movie is a complete fiction with Steve Madden They say Steve Madden there was the hottest woman shoe person and we had the chance to take a photo That's just complete fiction Steve Madden came to me as a dead cold startup Steve was very young Okay, he was a wild kid brilliant, which is just a wild guy. All right He was he was coming out of rehab, right? He's sober now for like five 40 years He's sober many many years Steve right this one. He's very young is why I was right like we all were right and he approached me and Because he was best friends with my junior partner Danny the Jonah Hill capture movie and Steve came to me with He wanted I want to build the shoe company He had no business plan He had nothing tangible, right and he just I'm gonna be the biggest, you know shoe guy out there All right, and I'm like all right Well at that point in time because I was in a situation where I had the ability to create What's called an exit ramp for anything I would have given anybody money So there was other reasons why Steve was able to raise money But here's the deal because he didn't even have a business plan drawn up He ended up giving away the store now There's many reasons why Steve got a very very bad deal at me All right, you got a poor deal and to my own discredit because it ended up causing me problems down the road That's a separable deal. We talk about negotiation Okay negotiating venture capital and negotiation in general But the point was that Steve Madden came to it with nothing just an idea. I Like here was this idea. I can I'm a great I know what a girls want to wear I want to build shoes and I want to just fucking make him and I'm gonna sell them everyone's gonna love my shoes I'm gonna be the next Sam and Libby except with a big brand back then right the bankrupt now, right? and it's like all right and You know he goes I said how much you need he goes a million dollars So I said all right. I'll give you a million dollars. So watch so I gave Steve He goes I need one million. I gave Steve one million dollars So Steve formed the company and put the rights into Steve Madden. I didn't give Steve a million dollars I gave the company a million dollars and I took 85% of the stock of Steve Madden shoes I took 85% leaving Steve with 15 and Steve was like thank you so much for this million dollars. Thank you. I can't believe you so grateful Guess what I? Would have done this for 50 50 Or 40 60 I mean I see Steve broke all these different rules and The main one that he broke by in it in this context was he had just an idea He didn't even bother Taking the idea Turning it into business a business plan and by doing that He could have projected it out on paper if he was right what that idea will be worth And I would have said it would have been worth, you know, it would be worth ten million dollars in three years And maybe I would have given him 50 50 for a million dollars Because he missed this step He had to give away this door. There was other reasons why to but those are not they're just incidental for this discussion Okay, I'll get to those later. So again, so watch now what happens So this is an important lesson here when you have an idea matter what it might be Even it's just for someone to give you money to for you could so you could purchase leads or you could hire a coke or whatever It might be before you approach that person someone to give you money take the idea Write it down on paper and turn it into either a short form business plan some sort of plan Preparately a regular business plan and by doing that you increase the value of the idea dramatically by as much as five to ten That is a must-do Step when it comes to raising money now Again, as I said before No one is going to give you money Because you write a good business plan at the end of the day It's gonna come down to your ability to explain remember that X Factor you explain to sell that plan To someone that's gonna be what gets you the money But this is what allows you to get a decent deal and not get your eyeballs ripped out That's part of the equation and for that matter most people won't even take you seriously Without a business plan. They would just they won't even look at it because it's just it's too half-baked All right, so that's the first way we are able to increase the value of What we have by essentially taking an idea and writing a plan Now let's say we want to go to the next step. What else could we do? Well, the next step would be would be to create what's called a prototype Let's say for instance now you were in some sort of business where simply you could show me What you are actually selling you could take you could take a little bit of bootstrapping You could create what's called a prototype. So essentially what you do by and remember the the idea always is the rule of thumb is anything you can do to make the business more real and by more real I mean less likely to fail or never get off the ground you increase the perceived value of the company So let's go back to Steve Madden Ultimately what happened was is Steve took some of this money that we gave him as a million dollars And by the way, just so you know because Steve Gave me such a huge chunk of the company gave me 85% for one million dollars What did I do the next day? I turned around and sold off 35% of that for one million dollars and Ended up with 50% of the company for free. I Laid off 35% of my 85 for the same thing. I paid that's such a good deal because he didn't know better Okay, now wasn't good for you to do it because it Came back to hold me later because he was so angry when he got wise so I don't recommend you do these things It was a mistake. I made very young. I was too good at negotiating I didn't realize that in a winning negotiation both sides have to walk away feeling good What has to be fair at least okay or else it comes back to bite you in the butt, okay, but that being said okay What Steve then did? He's he took some of the money and he turned them made a product They made a shoe called the Marilyn his first shoe and with that shoe We was able to go out and get it made in a local shoes show shoe manufacturer like an old-fashioned cobbler He started selling it to stores Okay, and what happened was guess what? People liked his shoe and they started selling through so what happened was the shoe guy this little shoe makes thieves Well like 40 penny he put them in stores on consign and people bought them they bought more That is called getting traction Traction means you don't really have a full blown business But you've actually shown that the idea you have actually will sell in the market places some demand for it You're getting traction you're showing that there's the man for it in the marketplace Okay, so now watch what happens here. So let's say the original idea is worth. I said Let's call it look all $500,000 right another hundred thousand dollars, right? Do you make it with the business plan now is worth? 500k by developing a prototype That it makes it more I say wow he's got a fucking the guy actually can make shoes Wow, that's pretty cool or someone shows me like the basic program They have an idea for a new app and they show me an app. It's not really working. I show them Wow, this apple going they can get that far it now makes the prototype worth One million dollars so in other words at a level of prototype I'll pay more for the idea So as I was saying, okay, so you take an idea now, right? That was now worth of a million dollars at the prototype because I say oh wow it actually is real as they can do that And then imagine if in that same prototype They put it in the app store and while the he hadn't had enough money to really buy any Advertising but he showed that he actually got it into the app store a few people bought it He made a couple of dollars. That's called having traction and When someone gets trash like oh Mike wow there is actual demand for the product it now is worth 2.5 million dollars worth even more and then the next level of traction is you actually launch it and you develop an actual running driving growing Business and at that level that's what it reaches essentially full value of the idea Which could be five to ten million dollars So in other words by taking an idea So the question I always get rules, you know, when should I raise money and what can I do to raise money? Well, the answer is is you have to say okay. Well, I have this idea how far Can I take the idea on my own by begging borrowing and stealing with I don't really mean stealing though, right? There's can you bootstrap? Can you put together a few bucks? You can certainly Always do this. That's just hard work in elbow grease There's templates on the internet and if you really can't write you get so for a couple hundred bucks to write one for you You could always take your idea and turn it into a business plan Everyone can do that. Okay. Now the next stage up would be can you actually develop a prototype? Well, it depends on what your idea is, you know, it's based on if it's a tangible or it's not a tangible idea But how far you have to ask yourself and you know reasonably speaking how? far can I get on What amount of money that I currently have that I'm wanting that I can you know reasonably risk and that's gonna be your sweet spot When you decide to go to someone and ask them to give you money Does that make sense? So that's what you're all in this is just the introduction to raising venture capital So you approach someone with an idea you're gonna get your clock cleaned You're gonna get very little for your idea Next stage up you would turn that idea into a business plan and then from there to a prototype Then they're going forward to a to get traction and onto a business. Okay, so now let me take you going further Right, so then I'm gonna just give you get we're gonna go broad here And then every once a month or so once every you know six weeks I'll introduce another layer of this here and I'm always gonna keep the VC training here related to like you know Gorilla VC tactics how will actually apply to you right so watch what happens now Let's say you actually go out and you go through this process and you're able to actually find someone to give you money So into rate you so it actually says yeah, I'll be a $500,000 and you raise money, right? Okay, let's talk about what happens next how you actually would one part that's really any important aspect of a How you're gonna get the money, but also ultimately what you're looking to do here, okay at the heart of all Venture capital investments is something called the exit ramp Okay, and I don't really I don't I don't want to see I don't really about ten minutes left here 15 minutes left here of training here before I and I'm gonna do the Q&A separately because I'm still not I'm still recording right now, right? So I don't want to get into the whole idea of explaining Essentially each individual pause. I'm gonna go broad for right now, but I want you to understand that and When someone in gives you money except the picture daddy or mom, okay? They don't invest in you Because they expect you to build a business and then start paying them dividends In other words essentially what drives the stock market the overall stock market is Is the value that when you invest money in a company that one day they will pay you dividends Mean that you'll get like you another established company if a stock is ten dollars a share and they pay a dollar Dividend every year. That's a 10% dividend that means every share you want you get back one dollar a year from the way It's go getting paid in dividend Most most growth companies don't pay a divin they use the money that they make you don't distribute it back to the shelves But the point is is that when someone gives an adventure capitalist or an investor an angel investor gives you money They're not expecting you to build a business that makes cash flow and let's say I end up buying 20% of the company Let's say my investment gets me 20% of your company My reason for doing that is not because I think you're gonna build a company that makes five million Profit a year and I get to a million dollars a year back in perpetuity. That's not why I invest in you So if you explain this idea To someone say listen, I'm gonna build a ex company and I'm gonna be we'll make five million And you'll be able to get a million dollars a year. I'll say thank you. I'm not interested people don't invest their money in ideas and companies like this because they expect to get dividends back to get Royalties to get profits each year. It's not the reason people invest when the typical angel investor Puts his money into a company. He's looking for his exit ramp. He's looking for a cash out meaning that the company is gonna do typically one of Three things Okay Number one the company is gonna become so successful that it becomes gets an IPO It goes public what that means is the company grows to a point where it can actually file with the Securities and Exchange Commission in the United States, right? If it's the US deal, right and that company goes public and starts trading on the stock exchange at which point you get What's called a liquidity event? In other words, everyone's looking for a liquidity event I'm not investing in you because I expect to get back My percentage of the profits each year doesn't work this way. That's not what angel investors do. That's what people who Work at the company. So if you if someone is your partner and they're working there Yeah, they'll invest because they they plan on running the company a building that that's a very just understand the mindset Don't ever approach someone and ask them for an investment because you're gonna pay them back each year out of the profits That's not what's gonna make them want to invest What they want to see is either a the companies have become so successful That it will list on a public stock exchange at which point you'll have freely tradable shares and they can then sell their shares They're 20% or some of those shares and make a bundle a hundred times their money That's the dream that everyone has when they invest in a small company and Order for that dream to be realized you have to fucking hit it out of the park That's why the story that you tell to someone it can't be about I have this company and Right now we're doing a hundred thousand a year and in four years now We'll be doing two million dollars will make 500 grand a year and you'll be getting back 75 grand a year rest of your life Let's say this good luck and not for me. That's not people are looking for home runs Venture capital your when you're a venture capitalist you are in the home run business You have no interest in base hits if you hit a series of base hits you will go bankrupt as a venture capitalist Why because and I'm just making up these numbers and they vary Depending on what industry but if you invest in ten deals as a VC Three of them are gonna go bust Yeah, three will go bankrupt another five Might end up okay. You'll get your money back make maybe make a small profit and then that one or two every time We'll go to the fucking moonshot and make you a hundred or two hundred times your money So while they say I'd like to get back several to ten times my money It typically doesn't work like what happens very often They just have one massive hit and a bunch of other losses it ends up being that in other words When someone gives you money, they're looking for a huge win Because they know the wind has to be substantial enough to offset the losses Okay, so the story and I'm gonna get to this in a separate train that we do I'll wait back you know again. I waited about a month I want to focus back on the sales stuff next week, right? But this is something you gotta know because it'll help you take your life to the next level, right? So the point is is if you tell the story of a double or triple and they're not interested They want to see the home fucking run So when you're doing your numbers and when you're running your projections, you gotta think big You have to really use that ability that I always teach you about focusing on the future You know when you make that vision, it's got to be bold and bright and clear and you have to assume that you have all the Capital you need and the skills you need to attract great people and build a massive company. That's what people want That's what they invested. They don't invest in basics and they don't invest in doubles. They want home runs Okay, because they know they need home runs in order to Make up for the losers that they will certainly see So that's called liquidity again for an IPO The other possibility, okay is you might not have an IPO, but you might get bought out Another company a public company typically will get bought out by a endless you shares That's when they get a liquidity at all They might pay cash for the company at which case everyone again gets essentially bought out of their investment and Makes a big score Okay, that's the second way that you get a liquidity event Now the third way you do this which is a lot more common than an IPO is what's called a reverse merger And a reverse merger is what you're gonna be hearing all and by the way 95 98% of the time nowadays Most of the deals that are going public are actually Happening through reverse merges into what's called shells and believe it or not This is something that I was probably one of the I was one of the not maybe not the inventive But the perfecter of the shell game back in the 1980s and I was the biggest player on Wall Street at doing reverse merges into shells Seriously, I was number one in the world of this So I know this business better than anybody and what this means is where you take your company You take an entity that you have a cut so your entities xyz It's private and you find a public shell Which means a company that's public, but there's no active business in it They went bankrupt the business went out business. They lost their money. So this is a public. It's still public though It's still registered with the security exchange commission called the 12g Reporting company and then what happens is you merge your company into this company? So let's say there might be a million outstanding shares and they'll issue you back 50 million shares So essentially you dilute out all these shareholds and you become the largest shareholds by doing that You essentially merge into the shell the shell is the surviving entity corporate entity and the shell changed its name to your company So you had a brand name. So let's say your company was called J RB global not had some value to it So you J RB global would merge into a shell and the shell would change its name to J RB global But the shell is the surviving entity Legally and those shares that you got issued when you merged in are now public shares So that person that gave you money that 20% they can then sell those shares and that's how they get the liquidity event It's called the reverse merger into a shell. It's very it could be it could work out well It also could be very sleazy and dangerous. You got to be very careful about that separate conversation Now the other way there's one other way that people also do get liquidity and that is sometimes you have a Management buyout where if the company's doing really well the madness is very rare these days, right? But occasionally you will have when a management will come in they'll from a bank financing and they'll take you out That happens not that often but it does happen So you can then get your money back as an investor they buy you out by a bank financing or someone else comes in and takes you out And you can get out that way. Okay, and that's your basic Introduction to venture capital now. I want to just give you before we how long we going to find out about Serious how long we go so long Okay, good. So let me keep going want to give you one more point here, okay, and that is some Tips here how not to get your clock clean, okay? and The first thing is this is by the way something and Just so you know because you're on this platform you're part of this program I would really expect that before you went out and went through this process You would at least post and let me know you're doing it So I then tell me what you're doing so I I just say hey that doesn't make sense That does because you get destroyed doing this but someone like Steve Madden like you went into blind flying blind without a mentor And you know and here was the mistakes you made. I'll try just very quickly list them number one is Know how much money you need Know the amount Steve Madden came to me and asked for a million dollars why? Because he thought it sounded like a good number he didn't need a million dollars. He could have done with a hundred grand Don't raise more money than you need because you dilute yourself out You got to give away Equity for that part of your company So don't raise more than you need and then conversely don't raise too little Because you don't want to do is raise money They'd be broke again in three months and then you know where so be careful you to think that through and that's why It's so important to have that be playing that business plan because the business plan walls gonna change in all three never gonna stick to it Exactly at least give you some right there feel how much money do you really need and what are you gonna use that money for? the last thing you want to do is Go to someone like myself a venture capitalist and raise money and then oops Sorry, I was I needed another half a million. I was wrong And you never you're gonna get destroyed You know they'll dilute you out terribly if they give you anything at all So you don't want to do that But what you do want to do is that you can actually say I'm gonna raise X That won't take me to this spot in the in my growth And I'll need that a series a second round a series B This is series a series B So you do multiple rounds of financing as long as it was part of the plan and then each round is Done a much higher valuation So if you raise a quarter million dollars in the first round and give away this much You can raise a million and not give away any more because you made that first quarter million Took the coming to a much higher value. She made it more valuable So you got to know how much to raise don't be scared to raise what you need But don't over raise because you dilute yourself out. That's number one number two another big thing here is No, the landscape Don't and this is what I said don't don't do this without at least letting me know you're doing it does Don't just go out there without understanding what a good deal looks like You show me a deal and I could tell you what it's worth more or less pretty quickly In other words, I could tell you what's a good deal What's a bad thing to do in my whole life and every Sophisticated investor does this can within reason we could be wrong sometimes right? I always remember you'll know within there's certain norms here of what makes sense and what makes absolutely no sense Steve Madden for him to go and I'm hopping on see he's done very well for himself I God I'm happy for him. I'm saying when he was back when he was naive now He's very sophisticated lesson right the point is I don't want you to have to go through that same thing Don't go into a negotiation of how much you're gonna give away of your company Without speaking to someone who knows the right answer first This is the biggest mistake that people make when they get into any negotiation They don't even know what a negotiation will look like a good negotiation looks like they don't know what a win looks like They either ask for something ridiculous and don't get the money or ask for too little and get destroyed There is a right sweet spot in there and I want you to know what that sweet spot is So ask not me as someone else who knows don't go into a V situation Floor VC flying blind because you're gonna get yourself destroyed probably unless you're very get very lucky. That's number two and number three One thing I want you to focus on focus on here is something called an earn out Well bonuses in other words very often you have a situation when you're asking someone to give you money is that while You have to give away X amount of the company now You can build in certain provisions that if you hit certain milestones if you succeed Then you could earn part of the company back They're called earn outs. So maybe you had to give away 30 or 40 percent, but if you hit certain Positive milestones, you know X valuation you get to earn part of your company back You can earn 10% back. So I want you to always remember your earn outs, okay? And again, this is just an introduction to raising money for venture capital But I want you to look at it this way. I don't most important I don't want you to ever think That you're limited in your greatness and your ability to achieve greatness because of having a lack of money There is always money available for good ideas For those who know how to explain them and close the deal and That's why it's on this platform because being on this platform that means you know how to do that You know how to close you know how to present you know how to get there a poor You're not scared to talk to people and ask them and tell them about your deal Don't go through life Thinking you have to always be the person that just works for someone else You have to always be the person that simply you know And it doesn't have to quit your job, but you can't raise money to build a business within the business you work for Think big Don't always be a paycheck player. I want you to just understand that you're not limited by the Confines of your own pocketbook or what you have or don't have Once you know the straight line system, you're learning it every day now once you know it a world of possibility opens up for you Because you have the ability to confidently go out there and tell your story To take an idea Turn it into a business plan get some traction develop a prototype if you can right start a business Or just grow a business when someone else's but don't think that you you're locked in Because you don't have enough money right now money is everywhere So while it's true that you typically do need some money to make money Doesn't matter because money is not in short supply The beauty of the straight line is it puts you in a position where you have a leg up on Everybody else who's looking to raise money for the same thing you are and there's usually a lot of people looking to raise money for a very similar idea Because the value of the sexiest what that idea is worth in someone else's mind always based on that first equation I told you about what's the intrinsic value? multiplied by your ability to tell the story and That's your introduction to raising venture capital guerrilla style Remember your capable of greatness you deserve greatness and Don't ever be held back by where you are right now You have an idea you need to put some money to that idea go out and fucking raise it I'll show you how it's your capable of it promise you all right.
